sections.text: Whenever your browser talks to a server, it needs to follow a set of rules so both sides understand each other. That set of rules is called HTTP, and its safer version is called HTTPS.

sections.title: What is HTTP?
sections.text: HTTP stands for HyperText Transfer Protocol. It is the language that browsers and servers use to talk to each other. It is just a set of agreed rules, like how to ask for a page, and how to send a page back. Without HTTP, your browser and the server would not understand each other at all.

sections.title: What is HTTPS?
sections.text: HTTPS is the same idea as HTTP, but with one important extra feature: it is secure. The S stands for Secure. HTTPS scrambles the information being sent so that nobody else watching the connection can read it. This matters a lot when you type in passwords or credit card numbers.

sections.title: How HTTP Requests and Responses Work
items: Your browser sends an HTTP request to a server, asking for a page.
The request includes details like which page you want and what type of browser you are using.
The server reads the request and prepares a response.
The server sends back an HTTP response, including the page content and a status code.
Your browser reads the response and displays the page on your screen.

sections.title: Think of it like Sending a Letter
sections.text: HTTP is like writing a letter using a normal envelope: anyone who handles it along the way could peek inside if they wanted to. HTTPS is like sealing that same letter inside a locked box: only the person with the right key, the server, can open it and read what is inside.
You can usually tell a website is using HTTPS by looking for a small lock icon next to the website address in your browser.

terms.definition: HyperText Transfer Protocol, the set of rules browsers and servers use to talk to each other.
A secure version of HTTP that scrambles information so others cannot read it.
A set of agreed rules that two computers follow so they can understand each other.
A short number a server sends back to tell the browser what happened, like 200 for success or 404 for not found.
A connection where the information sent between client and server is scrambled and protected.

questions.text: What does HTTP help your browser and a server do?
options: Talk to each other using a shared set of rules
questions.explanation: HTTP is a set of rules that browsers and servers both follow, so they can understand each other when sending requests and responses.

questions.text: What makes HTTPS different from HTTP?
options: HTTPS is much slower and old
HTTPS scrambles the information so it stays private and secure
questions.explanation: HTTPS adds security by scrambling the information sent between the browser and the server, so it cannot be easily read by anyone else.

questions.text: What can you look for in your browser to see if a site uses HTTPS?
options: A small lock icon next to the website address
questions.explanation: Browsers usually show a small lock icon near the address bar when a website is using a secure HTTPS connection.
